title: (More) Roses Amid Garbage and Trap Doors - Hussman Funds
gist: Hussman details an expanded 2026 hedging framework that lets his funds selectively turn 'constructive with a safety net' during extreme-valuation, unfavorable-internals ('garbage') conditions, revealing that a narrow ~19% slice of periods since 2021 accounts for the entire cumulative gain in the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100, while valuation metrics like MarketCap/GVA and tech price/revenue multiples sit at record extremes rivaling 1929 and 2000.

insights.claim: Hussman's investment discipline can vary the intensity of its downside hedges and, since February 2026, can also shift to a 'constructive with a safety net' stance even during periods historically classified as high-risk 'garbage' (extreme valuations plus unfavorable market internals), aiming to capture upside without abandoning downside protection.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Signals a structural shift in how the fund manages bearish periods - watch for more frequent tactical long exposure even while its headline outlook stays bearish.
insights.locator: (More) roses amid garbage and trap doors

insights.claim: Constructive 'rose' periods embedded within otherwise 'garbage' market conditions make up just over 2% of market conditions across history, but have occurred in close to 15% of weekly periods across the most recent 1-, 3-, and 5-year horizons, and these periods account for more than half of the S&P 500's total return over those horizons.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A tiny fraction of time windows drives the majority of index returns, so systematically identifying them (rather than being in or out of the market wholesale) is the core edge Hussman is claiming.
insights.quote: just over two percent of market conditions across history are roses
insights.locator: We know that conditions that join

insights.claim: Selloffs that occur amid very high bullish sentiment and low bearish sentiment are not genuine contrarian sell signals but evidence that speculators simply can't shake their optimism, and such selloffs are followed with striking regularity by fast, furious, but fragile ('prone-to-failure') rebound rallies.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Reframes lopsided bullish sentiment as a timing tool for entry rather than a pure warning sign - relevant for reading sentiment extremes during dips.
insights.quote: These selloffs are followed by ‘fast, furious, prone-to-failure’ rallies with striking regularity.
insights.locator: As an example – in certain conditions

insights.claim: Taken together, Hussman's discipline classifies about 64% of historical periods as constructive or strongly bullish overall, but only 40% of periods over the past decade.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Shows the recent decade has been unusually hostile to the fund's normal bullish criteria, context for why the fund has stayed defensive for so long.
insights.locator: Taken together, our discipline classifies about

insights.claim: Even though the market environment since 2021 has featured persistently unfavorable internals and extreme valuations, Hussman's expanded discipline still classifies 19% of that period as 'constructive' with a safety net - and that 19% slice comprises the entire cumulative gain in both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 since 2021.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Implies that outside this narrow 19% window, both indices have essentially gone nowhere on a cumulative basis since 2021 - a striking claim about how concentrated market gains have been in specific conditions.
insights.locator: What’s more important is that, even

insights.claim: In 2026 specifically, Hussman's discipline flagged only a single week in February and a single week in March as 'constructive,' together good for a 4.5% S&P 500 gain, versus the index's 7.6% year-to-date gain cited in the article.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Quantifies how much of 2026's rally the fund's systematic signals actually captured versus missed by design, useful for judging the strategy's realistic upside participation.
insights.locator: To be clear, yes, despite the market

insights.claim: Despite an S&P 500 rally of more than 15% since March 30, 2026, Hussman's discipline has shown zero new 'rose' signals in recent weeks, meaning the fund is deliberately not chasing the rally and is waiting for its next systematically-identified opportunity.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Explains why the fund remains hedged during a strong rally - it is a rules-based abstention, not a discretionary bearish bet against the tape.
insights.locator: You’ll note that we’ve seen no roses

insights.claim: The roughly 9% selloff from late January to late March 2026 was accompanied by too much bearish sentiment for Hussman's system to trigger a constructive shift, since historically similar levels of bearishness have been followed by 'very mixed' subsequent returns.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Clarifies the framework's asymmetry: it needs lopsidedly bullish (not bearish) sentiment alongside a selloff to classify conditions as a tradeable 'rose'.
insights.locator: Although we saw a brief market selloff

insights.claim: Hussman's MarketCap/GVA ratio (nonfinancial market capitalization to gross value-added, effectively an economy-wide price/revenue multiple) - which he calls his most reliable valuation measure by correlation with subsequent market returns across a century of cycles - is at the most extreme level in U.S. financial history.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: The author's core valuation gauge, historically correlated with long-run returns, is flashing its most extreme reading ever - a headline risk marker for long-term equity return expectations.
insights.locator: The chart below shows our most reliable

insights.claim: The elevated corporate profit margins that are propping up current valuations are the mirror image of record deficits among households and government - an accounting identity - so assuming margins stay permanently elevated implicitly assumes those deficits stay permanently elevated too.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Undercuts the bull case that high margins justify high valuations by tying margin sustainability directly to unsustainable-sounding deficit levels.
insights.locator: elevated corporate margins are the mirror image

insights.claim: Even earnings-based valuation measures, which are typically more conservative than revenue-based ones, now rival the valuation peaks of 1929 and 2000.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A second, independent valuation lens (earnings rather than revenue) confirms the same extreme reading, reducing the chance the signal is a measurement artifact.
insights.locator: even earnings-based valuations rival those of 1929

insights.claim: The information technology sector's price/revenue multiple and the Nasdaq 100's valuation are both at similarly steep, extreme levels alongside the broader market's valuation extremes.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Confirms the valuation extreme is not just an index-level artifact but concentrated in tech, the sector driving index gains.
insights.locator: We see another version of these extreme

insights.claim: Despite profound revenue growth for the largest technology companies since 2000, many of these companies still carry price/revenue multiples close to or above the levels they had in the late-1990s dot-com era - a red flag because valuation multiples should normally decline as rapid growth is realized, and that hasn't happened.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Directly challenges the narrative that mega-cap tech valuations are justified by fundamentals-driven growth, since the multiples haven't compressed the way growth realization should force them to.
insights.locator: for the largest technology stocks, valuation multiples

insights.claim: If valuation multiples aren't falling despite huge realized revenue and earnings growth, one of two things must be true: either the long-term rate of return investors expect has fallen significantly, or investors keep shifting the entire future growth trajectory forward in time, treating each period as a new baseline for permanently high growth - Hussman believes both dynamics are occurring simultaneously for the largest tech companies.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Provides a specific mechanism for why mega-cap tech can appear 'fairly valued' on naive growth-adjusted metrics while still being a bubble - investors are perpetually re-basing their growth assumptions.
insights.locator: If valuations aren’t falling, one of two

themes.theme: Market Internals as a 'Stubborn Optimism' Gauge

insights.claim: Since the interim market peak on January 4, 2022, the 'Magnificent 7' have gained 144% while the S&P 500 equal-weighted index has gained just 35% - a divergence Hussman cites as evidence that market internals have been persistently unhealthy since before the 2022-2023 market retreat.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Quantifies just how concentrated the market's advance has been in a handful of mega-cap names versus the median stock, a key input into Hussman's bearish internals read.
insights.locator: Observe, for example, that since the interim

insights.claim: Hussman's main research focus in recent weeks has been studying the individual sub-components that feed into his market-internals gauge, rather than just the aggregate signal, as an alternate proxy for 'stubborn optimism' - this allowed him to identify additional reliable 'roses' windows in February and March 2026 that the aggregate gauge alone would have missed.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Suggests the internals-based signal is being refined in real time; more granular sub-component analysis could surface future 'constructive' windows the headline gauge misses.
insights.locator: Studying these factors as an alternative proxy

themes.theme: Portfolio Construction and the Alternative-Sleeve Math

insights.claim: For any 'alternative' asset in a portfolio, not just individual stocks, an investor should only take an active position if its expected return exceeds what's implied by its market risk exposure - formally, ERa > Rf + beta(ERm - Rf); even a flat-return strategy can be a rational allocation if it has sufficiently negative correlation to the market.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete formula for deciding whether a hedged/market-neutral strategy deserves portfolio allocation even when its standalone return looks unimpressive.
insights.locator: Geek’s Note: See the last section

insights.claim: Since 2017, when Hussman began suspending bearish outlooks during periods of favorable market internals even amid extreme valuations, a blended portfolio split between the S&P 500 and Hussman's own investment discipline has achieved its maximum Sharpe ratio at roughly a 55%/45% allocation, and that blend has produced a higher return per unit of risk than the S&P 500 alone over that period.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete, backtestable allocation figure (55/45) for investors considering blending a hedged/alternative strategy with a passive S&P 500 holding.
insights.locator: we find that the Sharpe ratio

insights.claim: Hussman states the fund's explicit goal is for the optimal blended-portfolio allocation to shift toward an even higher weighting in its own discipline, and for the discipline itself to again outright outperform the S&P 500 as it did in market cycles before 'the current bubble' - implying he views recent underperformance as bubble-specific rather than a permanent state.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Signals the fund expects its relative performance gap versus the index to close once the current valuation extreme resolves, a claim investors in the fund should track over time.
insights.locator: Needless to say, our effort is for

themes.theme: Behavioral Framework: The 'Interbeing' of Risk and Opportunity

insights.claim: Hussman frames his entire hedging philosophy through a Buddhist 'interbeing' lens drawn from the teachings of Thich Nhat Hanh - that 'roses' (opportunity) and 'garbage' (risk/danger) are not separate, fixed categories but mutually contain and transform into each other depending on causes and conditions, which he presents as the deeper insight behind why extreme-risk market periods can still contain genuine constructive opportunities.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Explains the underlying mental model driving Hussman's willingness to find tactical long opportunities even inside conditions he otherwise labels dangerous 'garbage'.
insights.locator: Through most of this brief comment, I’ve

insights.claim: Hussman ties the fund's ability to be patient - neither chasing the bubble nor fearing a crash - to a personal practice of equanimity, describing a risk-conscious discipline that lets him look ahead 'without fearing a continued bubble' or 'a market crash' regardless of which way the market outcome goes.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Signals the psychological discipline underpinning the strategy's patience - useful context for investors trying to gauge whether the fund will capitulate under pressure during a prolonged rally.
